comments:
Summary
This paper presents an attentive convolutional neural network (ACNN) for speech emotion recognition and uses it to study (i) the impact of different acoustic input features (logMel, MFCC, prosody, eGeMAPS), (ii) the effect of signal length on performance, and (iii) differences between improvised and scripted speech. Experiments are run on IEMOCAP with leave-one-session-out evaluation; each configuration is repeated six times to report mean/min/max accuracies. The authors report state-of-the-art results on the improvised subset and provide an error analysis and discussion.

Strengths
- The problem is well motivated and relevant for HCI and affective computing.
- The experimental setup is thorough: multiple feature sets, three dataset splits (improvised/scripted/all), and experiments varying signal length. Leave-one-session-out cross-validation is appropriate for speaker-independence.
- Repeating runs with different random seeds and reporting mean/min/max improves reliability of results compared to single-run reporting.
- The paper provides practical implementation details (feature extraction settings, CNN hyperparameters) that aid reproducibility.
- The error analysis (confusion matrices and discussion of activation/valence confusions) is informative.

Weaknesses and suggestions
- Novelty is incremental: attention+CNN architectures and multi-view learning have been used previously. The main contribution is an empirical study rather than a fundamentally new model.
- Comparisons to stronger or more recent baselines could be expanded. For example, including a convolutional+recurrent baseline or other published CRNN/LSTM approaches with the same feature sets and experimental protocol would strengthen claims about competitiveness.
- The paper lacks ablation analyses that could clarify the contribution of model components: e.g., effect of the attention layer alone (without max-pooling), or performance without MV learning in more depth. Some of these are reported but a more systematic ablation would help.
- Statistical significance testing is not reported; given run-to-run variation (up to ~4% range) it would be useful to indicate whether reported differences are significant.
- The attention mechanism is not inspected beyond reporting small improvements. Visualizing attention weights or showing where the model attends for different emotions would provide useful insight and support claims about attention helping for longer/complex inputs.
- Some hyper-parameter choices (e.g., pool size 30, stride 3, dropout rate 0.8) are given but not justified. A short discussion on sensitivity to these choices or how they were selected would be helpful.
- Prosody features are only seven dimensions and perform poorly; the authors note this but a speaker-dependent experiment or richer prosodic set could confirm the interpretation.
- Potential issues from zero-padding shorter utterances (7.5s maximum) and truncation of long utterances are not discussed; padding effects could bias the model and merit comment.

Minor/editorial
- The paper text provided here contains duplicated fragments; I assume this is an artifact of the provided copy rather than the submission.
- Reporting per-class precision/recall/F1 (in addition to weighted accuracy) would make the evaluation more informative, especially given unbalanced class distributions.

Recommendation
I consider this a solid empirical study with useful findings for practitioners in speech emotion recognition. While it is not highly novel algorithmically, the systematic comparison across features, signal lengths, and speech types (improvised vs scripted), together with reproducible details, make it a worthwhile contribution. I recommend acceptance as a poster (or short paper) and suggest the authors address the ablation/visualization/statistical testing points in a revision.
